basmala: In the Name of God, the Infinitely Merciful, the Most Merciful.
intro: Al-Masad — “The Palm Fiber”, also al-Lahab, “The Flame” — is among the earliest Meccan sūras. It names Abū Lahab, the Prophet's uncle and one of his fiercest opponents. But a Sufi reading does not stop at the historical episode. Every soul carries an inner Abū Lahab — the part that mocks, that counts its fortune, that thinks itself safe from death as long as its goods hold. Tustarī explains: in the double “may he perish” of the first verse there are two losses — the loss of goods and the loss of soul. The first is inevitable and secondary; the second is what the sūra warns against. The cord of palm fibre that weighs upon the woman's neck, in this reading, is not only a posthumous punishment: it is the image of every bond that slander and hatred knot day after day around the heart — and which, when the hour comes, draw shut.

versets.trad: May the hands of Abū Lahab perish, and may he perish!
versets.commentaire: Tustarī distinguishes two movements in the double tabba (تَبَّ) — “may they perish”, “may he perish”. “The first loss is the loss of his wealth, and the other is the loss of his soul.” The hands, in Arabic, are the image of power and possession; when they “perish”, what stands outside the man collapses. But the second tabba reaches further: it is what is in him that perishes. The sūra turns on this passage from outside to inside: behind the loss of goods, the more secret loss of a soul that has tied itself to them. And Qushayrī, soberly: “Far be the one who has not known your worth, O Muḥammad. Far be the one who has not witnessed that with which We have honoured you.”

mots.def: tabba: to perish, to be lost, to see one's effort come to naught.
yad: the hand; in the dual, the two hands. Metonymy for power, action, what a man undertakes.
ab: the father; in the construct genitive: “of the father of”. Forms the kunya, the surname by sonship.
lahab: the flame, the blazing fire. “Abū Lahab”, “Father of the Flame”, the nickname of the Prophet's uncle.
coordinating conjunction: and.
tabba: to perish; here, in parallel to the opening verb, names not the hands (the having) but the man himself (the being).

versets.ar: مَا أَغْنَىٰ عَنْهُ مَالُهُ وَمَا كَسَبَ
versets.tr: mā aghnā ʿanhu māluhu wa-mā kasab
versets.trad: His wealth avails him not, nor what he has earned.
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī reports: Abū Lahab had said — “If what my nephew says is true, I shall ransom myself with my wealth and my children.” The verse answers. Mā kasab (مَا كَسَبَ), “what he has earned”, is read by several commentators as an allusion to his sons. The two main supports of a man in the world — money and offspring — are named and denied in the same breath. For Tustarī, behind this lies a universal saying: “The good (khayr) in truth is only in three things: to have no need of people and be independent of them, to acknowledge one's absolute poverty toward God, and to fulfil His commandments.” Every other wealth is, in the true sense, powerless.

mots.def: negative particle: not, not at all.
aghnā: to be of profit, to make independent of; with mā: has been of no profit.
preposition ʿan + suffix pronoun: from him, for him.
māl: wealth, possessions; with the suffix pronoun: his own wealth.
conjunction: and.
neuter relative pronoun: that which.
kasaba: to earn, to gain; some commentators also hear: his children.

versets.trad: He shall enter a blazing Fire,
versets.commentaire: Tustarī here lays out a Sufi typology: “There are four fires. The fire of desire (shahwa, شَهْوَة), which consumes acts of obedience. The fire of misery (shaqāwa, شَقَاوَة), which consumes the profession of Oneness. The fire of severance (qaṭīʿa, قَطِيعَة), which consumes hearts. And the fire of love (maḥabba, مَحَبَّة) — this one consumes all other fires.” The sūra does not only name the fire that awaits Abū Lahab. It also names, by counterpoint, the one fire that delivers from it: the fire of the love of God, which burns within the human being all that is not Him.

mots.def: prefix particle of the future attached to the verb: announces the coming action.
ṣalā: to enter a live fire, to endure its heat; here in the future, sa-yaṣlā.
nār: fire; in the indefinite accusative, the object.
dhāt: “possessing”; used to form a qualification: “endowed with”, “ablaze with”.
lahab: flame, blazing fire; the same word as Abū Lahab's nickname.

versets.trad: and his wife, the carrier of firewood,
versets.commentaire: The epithet ḥammālat al-ḥaṭab (حَمَّالَة الْحَطَب), “the carrier of firewood”, is in Arabic a common figure for the nammāma — the woman who carries slander. She feeds another's fire with her speech, until that very fire she helped to raise takes her in turn. A dense image: all that one carries to burn another's name is, unbeknown to oneself, fuel laid in one's own hearth. The sūra does not separate wife from husband in the punishment — not by family solidarity, but because the one who hates and the one who feeds the hate are caught in the same chain.

mots.def: conjunction: and.
imraʾa: woman, wife; with the suffix pronoun: his wife.
ḥammāla: intensive of ḥāmil; here, an idiom for the slanderer (nammāma).
ḥaṭab: firewood, fuel; figuratively, what feeds the fire of slander.

versets.trad: around her neck a rope of palm fibre.
versets.commentaire: The neck, in Arabic jīd (جِيد), is the noble part of the body, the place where ornament is worn. Tustarī recalls that in the literal tradition, it is a chain of iron she will bear in the Fire — like a pulley by which the rope is hoisted, identifying her there as her hostility identified her here. But in the spiritual sense, the rope of woven fibres speaks further: what the soul weaves day after day — envy braided with pride, slander braided with resentment — ends by making her a collar. The verse closes the sūra on this image: what one does, one wears. The cord is not knotted by anyone — it is knotted by oneself.

mots.def: preposition: in, on, around.
jīd: the neck; the noble part where ornament is worn. With the feminine suffix pronoun: her neck.
ḥabl: rope, cord, cable — figuratively, every binding that endures.
preposition: of, from; indicates the material from which something is made.
masad: woven palm fibre, used for coarse ropes; the rare word that gives the sūra its name.
